videos.transcript: hi guys my name is Anna and in this video I'm going to be talking about things to not do in Korea as a foreigner I'm doing this video because some people ask me what they should be mindful of while they're visiting Korea and before I start getting into the place let me just preface by saying that none of this is a hundred percent the Bible not every single Korean gets bothered or things this way so this is simply just my advice but it's not absolute set rule in Stern and there might not be any real consequences from you not following these things but just putting that out there for those of you who may not know I'm Korean I was born in Korea but I lived in the UK for a long time and I've also traveled a lot of the world so I've seen how other countries are and I think I can just give advice on things that might be okay over there compared to how things are in Korea so again not saying I know everything but just giving you guys advice so let's go so number one full-on extreme public displays of affection so things like making out or like grouping each other's private parts in public that is not okay like hugs are putting your arms around each other and like a quick Peck here and there that's completely fine but if I look her to Paris or even in London I see people just full I'm almost going at it in public that is not okay in Korea so avoid really extreme public displays of affection in a public space number two shoes obviously in most Asian countries you don't wear shoes in the house I think a lot of people already know this but also when I went to like New York Subway I would see people put their feet up on the chair in front of them on trains and in Korea is really not okay to do that that includes train seats or park bench seats just put your feet on the ground nowhere else basically that's the general rule so a lot of Korean buildings they actually have slippers you take your shoes off and then for the actual room they have slippers for people and I've seen some foreigners complain about this because they're like I don't want to take off my shoes I think my shoes look cute with this outfit I don't really want to wear these pink slippers or whatever but you have to do it you just have to follow the rules absolutely no shoes allowed in the house and not on public seats number three is is jaywalking and Crossing on a red light even if there are absolutely no cars around in Korea just follow the traffic lights when I lived in London if I go to New York I know everybody just jaywalks if there are no cars around everyone thinks it's fine that you just cross the road whatever but in Korea really don't do that I was even surprised at this because I was with my dad once and it was like really God early time in the morning like five or six a.m there were absolutely no cars right there and we just had to cross the road to get to the hospital it was the red light Crossing and I was like but Dad call me just walked there's like no one here he was like no in Korea we don't do that no no it's not okay so we waited until the light turned green even if no cars went by but yeah that's just how Koreans do it just follow the rules third jaywalk also there was an incident a while ago where two foreigners I think Americans they were skateboarding down the hills of etawan obviously as you go down you pick up speed but there was a truck driver coming from the other side and they both crashed and luckily nobody got massively injured from that incident but the driver might have PTSD he thought he might have killed someone so don't do kind of stupid stuff like this be respectful and use common sense because that could have easily happened and somebody really could have been injured or like died so yeah number four don't wait for the bill to come to your table at the end of the meal even I had to get used to this again after I came back to Korea in 2020. in London you have your meal and then the waitress she will bring your bill and even the card machine to the table and then you pay and then you leave but the problem with that is sometimes the waiter or the waitress they could be waiting on everyone else and you might not be able to pay for like 20 minutes half an hour so in Korea as soon as you're done with your meal there's a counter that you go up to you pay don't wait for the bill to come to you there are some restaurants where that might be an exception but I would say most of the cases after you finish you go you pay you leave I think this is more efficient way of doing things so yeah don't expect the bill to come to you number five we're going to talk about public transport because a lot of you guys foreigners use it when you travel to Seoul so don't sit on the priority seats in the train and those are when you go in through the carriage doors there are like three and three seats closest to the door and those are strictly reserved for the elderly so do not sit there and then you have like the long run of seats there's a whole general public and also pink seats are reserved for pregnant ladies so don't sit there unless you're pregnant talking loudly or listening to music very loudly and just drawing attention to yourself on public transport is generally a No-No people have usually their airpods or they're watching stuff on their phone if you're with a friend just talk on the quieter side and just chill on the train number six people ask me like is it still the case that it's inappropriate to show like chest cleavage in Korea I would say especially in regards to foreigners because I think people are way more lenient with foreigners they're not really going to care so much and it's because it's summer everyone in Korea kind of dresses in like short or revealing clothing as well but you might get stared at so if you feel uncomfortable with that if you're wearing like a low-cut top maybe bring like a cardigan or like a jacket to throw one for the train but like in the summer on the streets there are loads of people just wearing kind of what they want and people are asking me is it okay to show stomach I think it's fine there's loads of people wearing crop tops these days but like I said on public transport when a lot of elderly people are there as well maybe just bring a light covering for the trade there was one incident for me as well when I was on the train with my friend and I was wearing like a sidey crop top a pair of Dickies trousers they were kind of like trendy and like the top of the trouser rolls over and it kind of shows your belly button and like that midriff area it's like the elderly lady she like tapped me on my shoulder she's like I don't think you did your trousers up all the way she thought that I had just forgot to like zip them up completely to the top and I was like oh no this is how you're supposed to wear them it's fashion I think she's genuinely concerned for me that I had like left the house without remembering so in instances like that it's basically up to you but I don't think anyone is going to really give you a hard time about it but if you're just uncomfortable with people staring at you then that's just my advice obviously if you're in Korea for a long term and you actually like work here then in the workplace then obviously be slightly more professional and not wear too much revealing clothing but this is kind of a given in most countries I think so yeah just keep that in mind number seven again I preface this by saying Korean could be way more lenient with foreigners but in Korea if you're handing something to someone it's polite to give it with both hands instead of just going like this and you also accept with both hands so when you receive or giving things just try to remember to do with both party most Koreans will find it like really Charming if you do that because they won't really expect you to know but they will find it Charming if you do this so that's my advice number eight I've seen some people that I know do this when they come to Korea don't do the Namaste gesture when you bow in Korea because that is not a Korean custom we don't do this that's used in India and parts of Southeast Asia but in Korea we just we just bow we don't do this hand gesture so yeah that is not a Korean custom so don't do that number nine do not leave a tip at restaurants it's not a thing here like in America for example nobody thinks you're rude if you don't leave a tip finish your meal just pay what you owe they will give you back change if you need change don't leave a tip number 10 don't get offended when people ask you for your age in the west if somebody asks you like how old are you that can sometimes come off as quite rude but in Korea there is an age hierarchy system and everyone just asks each other for their age because obviously Koreans we use honorifics and how you address them and stuff so it is important to figure out someone's age so don't be offended if people ask you for your age like kind of immediately after asking for your name that's just a thing in Korea number 11 don't expect like Greetings or small talk with random strangers because in Korea everyone pretty much keeps to themselves and also making like direct eye contact with people on public transport it's not really a thing like when I went to New York last time people would just start talking to me on the street hey how's it going and just stuff like this in Korea it's just really basically never happens and also this is tied to number 12 but if some people do come up to you and try to make small talk be mindful that it could be a cult trying to recruit you so number 12 is don't join a cult because like 100 of the time is not going to be beneficial for you so if somebody approaches you on the street and it's like oh you can get like a free Fortune reading or you can join this club where you can make friends just ignore them and just keep walking because yeah don't join a cult and also don't try to hug someone upon the first meeting Koreans do not hug and create in a social situation if a friend introduces you to a new friend don't agree like hey what's up and hug that is not a thing just here and sometimes I do like handshake but generally No hugs between Koreans when you first meet someone okay and number 13 not saying everybody but some Koreans especially like the elderly they can be quite sensitive in regards to Japan because Korea was under Japanese rule from 1910 to 1945 so things like the Rising Sun flag which is different from the national flag of Japan the Rising Sun flag that is actually an Imperial War flag and sometimes you see that on pieces of clothing or on bags or on stickers so just be mindful of the fact that some Koreans may find that disrespectful because it is like a war flag party nobody's gonna come up to you and be like la la la but just something to maybe just keep in mind because I know a lot of westerners don't even know about like the Rising Sun flag and the meaning so this is just me letting you know and lastly number 14 cat calling I have personally never seen or experienced men cat calling in South Korea I've never seen it but I have experienced it in London in America and other Western countries obviously it's just generally a rude thing to do even back in those countries women don't like it there but it's just simply not done in South Korea so if you're a foreigner if you're partying so and you just get drunk and you just suddenly do cat calling or just like saying stuff to strangers just avoid doing that and you'll be all good so those were my 15 points let me know if you guys have anything you want to add or you want to ask please like And subscribe and also turn on the Bell notifications so you don't miss out when I upload new videos thank you for watching and I'll see you in the next one bye [Music]

videos.title: From Incheon to Seoul: The CHEAPEST, FASTEST & EASIEST Ways (2025 Guide)
videos.description:
Planning your trip to Seoul? 🇰🇷 The first step is figuring out the best way to get from Incheon International Airport (ICN) to the city. In this 2025 Korea travel guide, I break down the 4 main transportation options to help you choose the right one for your budget and travel style.

Whether you're looking for the cheapest, fastest, or most hassle-free way to start your adventure, this video has you covered. I'll compare the pros and cons of taking a Taxi, the Airport Limousine Bus, the AREX Express Train, and the All-Stop Subway. Plus, I'll explain the difference between a T-money card and the WOWPASS so you can pay for it all!

---

⬇️ **TIMESTAMPS** ⬇️

00:00 - Intro: 4 Ways to Get to Seoul

00:23 - Option 1: Taxi (The Hassle-Free Choice)
The easiest, most comfortable option, especially for families or those with heavy luggage. Learn where to find the taxi stands and what to expect for the cost.

01:52 - standard public transportation card vs. WOWPASS
Before you take public transport, you'll need a payment card. I explain what a T-money card is and break down the WOWPASS (a tourist-exclusive prepaid debit card) – including where to buy it at the airport and a key tip for using it.

04:15 - Option 2: Airport Limousine Bus (Comfort & Value)
A great balance of comfort and price. The bus has comfortable seats and takes you to various drop-off points across Seoul, making it a very convenient choice.

05:04 - Option 3: AREX Express Train (The Fastest Route)
The quickest way to get directly to Seoul Station from Incheon. Perfect for travelers who want to save time.

05:43 - Option 4: Subway / AREX All-Stop (The Budget Choice)
The cheapest method to get into the city. I'll explain how it works and give you a crucial tip for avoiding rush hour crowds.

06:24 - Final Thoughts & Summary

---

**ABOUT GAMJA IN SEOUL:**
Welcome to the channel! I'm a local from Korea dedicated to showing you the real Korea. Join me and my puppy, Gamja, for practical travel tips, virtual tours, and insights into Korean culture.

✅ **If this video helped you plan your trip, please give it a LIKE and SUBSCRIBE for more Korea travel guides!**

**Which method will you be using on your trip to Korea? Let me know in the COMMENTS!**

Music: Local Forecast - Slower by Kevin MacLeod

You don't need to take any escalator or elevators. You just walk out to the gate. [Applause] So taxis are all right here. As you can see, the taxi stands here have platform numbers. One thing that might be confusing is that a different platforms serve different regions in Korea. For example, platform 6C over there goes to Goang and Bhan, while 5D serves different parts of Inchan. It's best to know your destination beforehand to find the right platform. But don't worry too much. If you go to the wrong one, the driver will simply point you to the correct stand. The taxi fair depends on the distance and traffic conditions. But a trip into Soul shouldn't cost you more than 100,0001, which is roughly $75. To be honest, taking taxi is probably the easiest way for you to go to your destination. It'll cost you a lot compared to other um options that I'm going to show you later. If you're willing to pay more money for extra comfort and less stuff to think about, just take taxi. Easiest. For getting around, you have a couple of main payment options. When you buy and top up a standard transportation card at a convenience store, please remember that you can only use cash for that transaction. You might be wondering why. Are you having a hard time choosing your accommodation in Seoul because there are too many options? I understand. When I was living outside of Seoul, I had a hard time choosing accommodations every time I visited Seoul. I went and stayed in many different places here and there. And now I'm in Seoul and I do recommend locations to my friends who are visiting Seoul. So in today's video, I'm going to share with you my top 3 locations where to stay in Seoul. Also, I'll share with you where Koreans choose to stay for their vacations. But first, open the map of Seoul. I recommend using Google Maps. Open Google Maps and pin all the places where you want to go in Seoul. I have pinned it like this. These are the places where I think tourists might want to visit while traveling Seoul. As you can see, there are a lot of pins in the upper middle part of Seoul. And here are some orange pins and yellow pins. The color doesn't matter much so don’t mind it. I purposely done it to separate the neighborhoods. The three places where the pins are gather are near Hongdae, Myeongdong, and Gangnam. Yes. These are places you probably heard a lot in other blogs or videos as recommended places to stay in Seoul. Now you get why those blogs, videos and I are recommending these places. Because attractions are located nearby and the transportations are much comfortable here. So when you are choosing an accommodation in Seoul, it is better to choose the upper part of the river rather than the lower part, and try to go around these three locations. Now you might ask from these three, which one is the best? For that is really up to you to choose the location that suits you. Hongdae is the closest location to the airport out of the three. So it is a place where you can come easily by taking AREX from Incheon Airport. However, it is a little away from other attractions. It takes about 20 to 30 minutes to Myeongdong area and 40 minutes to an hour to Gangnam area. Since Hongdae is in the university district, there are many cheap restaurants and street shopping places and they are generally cheaper than other places. There are many foreigners and many staffs speaking English and understand English so you can visit without worrying too much about speaking Korean. However, Hongdae has the young vibe full of students, so it's crowded and can be noisy until dawn. I recommend Hongdae for those who are looking for a location close to the airport, an affordable place, or the young vibe of Korea. Myeongdong is the closest location to other attractions among the three. If you are planning to visit many attractions, Myeongdong is a good place to get your accommodation. Also it’s between Hongdae and Gangnam, so you can go both sites easily. There is no direct way to Myeongdong by taking AREX from Incheon Airport, so you have to transfer one's at Seoul station. Alternatively, you can take a bus and go straight to Myeongdong from Incheon Airport. Myeongdong is also known as a tourist town, because there are many tourists staying there since this is very convenient location for foreigners to stay. There are old restaurants that are good and many shopping malls, street and brand stores Some downsides are it’s a bit messy and don't have much of Korea feel, Korea vibe, and it's a little expensive. It is recommended for those who want to stay nearby the attractions and want to surrounded by English. Gangnam is far. It is far from the airport and far from attractions. However, because it is a place with many trends, there are restaurants, cafes and hot places everywhere. This is not a place where foreigners gather much, so language could be a little burden compared to Hongdae and Myeongdong. Also, Gangnam is expensive. So I personally don't recommend Gangnam as a place to stay except for those who want to visit trendy places in Gangnam area. If I were to stay in Seoul for a week or so, I will probably stay in Gangnam for a day or two to go to attractions near Gangnam area. But also going back and forth from Myeongdong to attractions in Gangnam area is also doable. By the way, I am referring to Gangnam as southeat part of Seoul, including Gangnam, Apgujeong, and Jamsil, not Gangnam Station alone. I went with three places that I recommend the most in Seoul. These places are famous places where Koreans also visit to hang out. So these locations are always crowded but safe. Also, these locations aren't like residential area, so you can't really experience the Korea living culture. There are also other locations such as Itaewon or Dongdaemun, but I wouldn't recommend them if this is your first time visiting Seoul. Because Itaewon don't have good transportation and Dongdaemun, rather to choose Dongdaemun, I'll probably choose Myeongdong because there are close by. Now, I'll tell you in top three locations where Koreans go for their vacation or staycation or holiday-cation or whatever for special cases. First is Yeouido. Yeouido has the luxurious hotels, expensive hotels. So people go there for their special events. They can enjoy pool, they have great Han River view. Sometimes people go there to party in an expensive hotel. Second place is Namsan, Seoul station. There aren't that many, but people go there especially in spring and fall foliage season to see the amazing view with N Seoul Tower. So that’s two. Third is Myeongdong. Koreans go there because there are a lot of hotels in different price range. There is a lot of option that they can choose from which that suits them. Also, there are a lot of attractions that people can visit during their stay in Myeongdong. So Yeouido, Namsan, and Myeongdong are top three places. I'll be doing a part two of where to stay in Korea to show you guys around other places, other locations so that you can see what other options are there too. Types of accommodations. My friends asked me which one to choose. Hotels, Airbnbs or guest house. Hotels are most expensive. If you can afford hotel, then I'll probably go with hotel. But if you think hotel is a little expensive, then you can choose Airbnb because with the price of hotel, you can get much nicer place with Airbnb. Guest Hosue is the cheapest. You can probably get one for like 20 bucks a night or something. But downside is that you have to share a room with other people. This is it for today's video. It is very difficult to choose accommodation when traveling. Once again, I'll be doing a part two of where to stay, showing you around other places so that you can see which other options there are. Also, I'm thinking of visiting hotels and other accommodation myself and give you all the reviews. If there is a hotel you like to know about, please leave a comment and I'll try to go there first. Thank you so much for watching. And please give it a like and leave us a comment to support us. And don't forget to subscribe our channel for more Korea travel tips. I'll see you in the next video. Thank you. Bye.

videos.transcript: What's up, guys? We're here in Seoul, the vibrant capital city of South Korea. And in this video, I'm going to show you our top things to do here. So, hope you enjoy it. Seoul is located in the northwest of South Korea. It's an immense city with the greater metropolitan area making up over half of the country's entire population. This is a busy yet rewarding destination, offering everything from magnificent royal palaces to traditional Hanok villages, a vibrant culinary scene, and tranquil natural spaces. In this video, we'll highlight the best things to do in Seoul for first-time visitors. Make sure to watch until the end as we'll also share essential travel tips to make your trip seamless. Now, let's dive in. We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Gangnam is located on the south side of the Han River. It's one of the most recognizable districts in the city, largely thanks to the world famous song Gangnam Style. This area is synonymous with luxury, holding a reputation as Seoul's most exclusive neighborhood. One of the highlights is visiting the visually stunning Starfield Library inside the COEX Mall. You should also take time to dive into K-pop culture at the K-Star Road, where you can find some photogenic bear statues. Indulging in high-end shopping on the surrounding streets is also a popular thing to do in this area. For a contrast to the modern vibe, you can explore the UNESCO-listed Royal Tombs, which are the burial mounds of three rulers and consorts from the Joseon Dynasty. These historical tombs are located within a lush forested park. If you're looking to take a break from the hustle and bustle, try visiting one of Seoul's green spaces. We really enjoyed the Seoul Forest, which is located to the east of the city center. This multi- themed park features a beautiful pond, a deer corral, vast open lawns, and tranquil walking paths. For an even more expansive option, head to the Olympic Park. This park was originally built for the 1988 Summer Games. Watching a drone show is a unique experience with over a thousand drones forming incredible images in the night sky. Performances are usually held seasonally, such as on select weekend nights in spring and autumn. When we visited in September, the show was held at Ttukseom Hangang Park, drawing a large local crowd. Check the Seoul Drone Show website or social media closer to your travel time for the most up-to-date schedule. But if your visit doesn't align with the same timet as a drone show, you can head to the Banpo Bridge for the Moonlight Rainbow Fountain Show. This spectacle takes place from April to October, generally running every evening from 7:30 to 9 with 20-minute shows occurring every 30 minutes. Renting a bike along the Han River is a great way to enjoy Seoul from a different perspective. The riverside boasts wide biking lanes which allow you to cover significant distances at your own pace. We did a scenic ride from Dangsan station to Banpo Hangang Bridge, which covers about 14 km or 8.6 m. The ride took us about 2.5 hours total. This was at a very relaxing pace, including a picnic stop along the riverbank and multiple river crossings. We timed our ride to start later in the afternoon so we could catch the first evening Light and Fountain show at Banpo Bridge. Renting is easy with the Soul Bike App, which is used to locate a nearby bicycle and then unlock it with a QR code. A 24-hour pass only costs about 5,000 Won. One of Seoul's most famous and popular neighborhoods is Myeongdong , serving as a vibrant shopping district. As a prime tourist hub, the area is constantly buzzing with hundreds of stores illuminated by shining neon lights. It offers a perfect mix of fashion, entertainment, and K-beauty shops that offer the latest trends. By late afternoon, the main street transitions into a dynamic night market where stalls offer delicious street food. Just be aware that the food prices here are higher than those found in more traditional local markets, which we'll talk about later in this video. Located in Seoul's city center, Insadong is known as the heart of Korea's traditional culture and art scene. The area has a long main street as well as winding side alleys, all lined with typical wooden-fronted shops. You can find a range of small boutiques and art galleries selling everything from modern paintings to ancient ceramics. There's also some hidden tea houses with great ambiances where you can enjoy a tasty cup. When we visited during the weekend, we were lucky to catch some cultural street performances. A day tour to the Korean Demilitarized Zone is a must-do experience. It offers a look into the most defining part of modern Korean history, which is the division of the peninsula. The DMZ was established by the 1953 Armistice and is a fortified buffer strip that's about 4 km or 2.5 mile wide. Our DMZ tour covered significant sites such as the Third Infiltration Tunnel and an Observatory with direct views of North Korea. While videos and photography weren't allowed inside the high security zone, we were allowed to document the visitor areas just outside of it. The trip concluded at the Red Suspension Bridge, providing a moving end to the history packed day. It's pretty difficult to visit this region of South Korea on your own, s we're definitely happy that we chose to experience it with a local guide who knew the area well, which brings us to the sponsor of today's video. GetYourGuide is the leading online travel experience platform with more than 110,000 curated things to do in 150 countries, from exclusive attractions to off-the-beaten path gems. In Seoul alone, there's over 450 experiences to choose from. All of these are rated and reviewed by verified travelers just like us. This means you can find activities matching your interests and book with confidence. Their user-friendly App keeps all your tickets and experiences organized in one convenient place. The DMZ specifically is a highly controlled military zone and independent entry is strictly prohibited by South Korean regulations. You can only visit with a licensed tour company which GetYourGuide provided many options for on their platform. The guide isn't only mandatory for managing security checks, but also provides contextual information about the divided Koreas, which is something we really appreciated. With 24/7 customer support and a flexible cancellation policy, GetYourGuide gives you the peace of mind to book confidently. The ability to modify plans up to 24 hours in advance ensures your itinerary can adapt to changing weather or preferences. Based on our experiences, we highly recommend GetYourGuide for your next adventure. Namsan Mountain is a prominent landmark situated at the southern edge of Soul's central Myeongdong district. You can ascend the mountain via numerous well-maintained walking tracks. Alternatively, you can take a quicker route by first riding the free inclined elevator to the lower cable car station. From there, you can buy a round-trip ticket to the top of the mountain. The peak offers phenomenal panoramic city views, which are especially popular at sunset when the city lights begin to sparkle. The N Seoul Tower can also be found at this summit, which you can ascend for even better views of the sprawling metropolis and the Han River. The tower complex also features other interesting areas to visit if you have time. These include the famous Locks of Love, free lower observation decks, and a small shopping zone. Hongdae is located west of Soul's city center and is short for Hongik University Street. Drawing energy from the university's art scene, this neighborhood is a magnet for young people as well as tourists. We made sure to experience this vibrant district during both the afternoon and at night. While the daytime offered great opportunities for shopping as well as cozy café breaks, we preferred the evening atmosphere. This is when the area truly came alive with neon lights, music spilling from bars, and people enjoying the countless game options in the local arcades. Two points. Three points. Cheonggyecheon Stream is a restored waterway in the heart of the city. It officially begins at a plaza near the Seoul City Hall and flows for 11 km or 6.8 miles before ending near the Han River. The most interesting and historic section runs from Plaza east toward Gwangjang Market. This is a comfortable walk of about 2 km or 1.2 miles one way. This section features small waterfalls, various art installations, and stepping stones as you stroll below the city streets. Seoul is home to five royal complexes which stand as monuments to the Joseon Dynasty that ruled Korea for over 500 years. The most famous is Gyeongbokgung Palace since it's the largest and most imposing of these complexes. Here you can find the vast Throne Hall and the stunning lakeside Pavilion. If you rent the traditional dresses called hanbok, you can enter the palace for free. Just a short walk to the east sits Changdeokgung Palace, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This palace is celebrated for its more organic design, and we found it to be a much more relaxed experience. When you are at the palace, we do recommends paying the extra fee to visit the Secret Garden because it's such a peaceful and lush forest setting. Also centrally located, Ikseon was our favorite neighborhood in the city and a wonderful area to stay. This historic district is defined by its maze of narrow streets and traditional Korean houses called hanok, which are now home to some of Seoul's hippest spots. The atmosphere is more intimate here with cozy restaurants, craft beer bars, and aesthetic cafés at every turn. We particularly love the local Korean barbecue options, some of which had outdoor seating in small courtyards. Trying the local dishes is an absolute must when visiting South Korea. If you're new to this cuisine, joining a food tour is the best way to tackle the endless options. We joined a tour to sample Gwangjang Market's finest, skipping the lines and enjoying an immersive cultural experience. This is one of Seoul's oldest and largest traditional street markets. Our host guided us to numerous local vendors where we tried a variety of dishes such as gimbap, savory pancakes, spicy tteokbokki, dumplings, bibimbap, and traditional rice wine. We also sampled some more adventurous dishes. These included chicken feet, beef tartar, and even live octopus. Okay, it's not moving anymore. You're eating it like a noodle. Located on the northern edge of the city, Bukhansan National Park is home to jagged granite peaks and dense forests offering some of the best hiking in the country. We tackled the popular Bukhansan Main Route. This point-to-point hike is about 7.6 km or 4.7 miles total with 761 meters or 2,496 feet of elevation gain. The trek starts at the information center, following paved paths past historic gates and temples. It then quickly transitions to a challenging mix of rocky terrain and endless stone steps. The final ascent requires holding on to metal poles and some ropes. Unfortunately, we couldn't reach the very top since the peak was closed for maintenance the same day that we were there. So, here's a stock photo of what it can look like if you make it to the summit. You can enjoy incredible panoramas of Seoul before descending toward the Bukhansan Metro Station. It can get really hot during summer, so plan on bringing a lot of water for the trek. A hanok village is a residential neighborhood composed entirely of Korean traditional houses with unique curved tile roofs and wooden structures. Seoul has several different ones. The most famous one is Bukchon Hanok Village, centrally located between the two palaces. Its winding streets offer awesome views of the modern city framed by historic rooftops. Just keep in mind that it can get quite crowded on the small streets here due to its immense popularity. But our personal favorite Hanok village was actually a newer one called Eunpyeong. Situated at the northwestern edge of the city, this village boasts a serene setting nestled directly under the granite peaks of Bukhansan National Park. We loved the atmosphere in the small cafes as well as the tranquil wooden walkways in the adjacent wildlife reserve. As we promised at the beginning of the video, here are three essential travel tips for Seoul. First, the city is huge, and while its public transport is excellent, spending less time commuting means more time sightseeing. For a first time visit, we highly recommend staying in a central area such as Myeongdong, Insadong, or Ikseon-dong. All of these neighborhoods are close to key cultural sites. We stayed in both Hongdae and Ikseon-dong and preferred the latter since it placed us in close proximity to the majority of the city's main attractions. However, Hongdae did seem to offer more budget accommodation options if that's the priority for your trip, and it's also well connected. The subway and bus system in Seoul is fast and incredibly well organized. You'll need a reloadable travel card for your visit, which can be purchased and topped up with cash at any convenience store such as CU or GS25, as well as at machines and subway stations. You should note that Google Maps doesn't work for transit directions in South Korea, so make sure to download and use Naver Maps to get around. They offer accurate real-time subway and bus routes, including which exit to take. Seoul's weather has dramatic changes from season to season. Avoid the summer from late June to August, which is marked by high heat, intense humidity, as well as the monsoon season that brings heavy rain. When we visited in early September, it was still quite hot and humid. For the most comfortable experience, target the autumn from October to early November or the spring from April to May for milder temperatures. Winter is very cold with average January lows often dropping below freezing. Thanks for watching and we hope you enjoyed it. You can find more videos about South Korea on our channel as well. These include more tips for exploring around different destinations. And if you're planning your trip to South Korea, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below the video. We also included all the important links you need for your Seoul experience in the description below this video. We'll catch you on the next adventure.

It's best to know your destination beforehand to find the right platform
Honestly, here's my personal recommendation
When you buy and top up a standard transportation card at a convenience store, please remember that you can only use cash for that transaction
If they accepted credit cards for it, the transaction fee from Visa or Mastercard would mean they actually lose money on every top up
If you choose to take the ax, you can choose either express or the all stop
My recommendation, I highly recommend you to take the express, not the all stop
As per commercial, express takes about 50 minutes from Incan airport to Seoul and the all stop takes about 70 minutes
But in reality, all stop actually takes longer than that
You can trust me on this because I used to commute with the all stop
For all stop, you don't have to take any other tickets
